agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "DynaPricing is a real-time dynamic pricing system for delivery services that adjusts prices based on demand levels. The CEO describes it at a high level with clear intent (demand-based pricing, automation, guardrails) but leaves most specifics undefined. This is a vision statement, not a specification.",
    "bounded_context": "Pricing",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "DynaPricing", "definition": "The product/system that performs real-time demand-based delivery price adjustments" },
      { "term": "Demand", "definition": "A measure of current order volume or request frequency relative to capacity, used as the primary input signal for price adjustments" },
      { "term": "Delivery Price", "definition": "The price charged to the customer for a delivery, which DynaPricing adjusts dynamically" },
      { "term": "Guardrails", "definition": "Upper and/or lower bounds or constraints that prevent prices from reaching extreme or unreasonable values" },
      { "term": "Real-time adjustment", "definition": "Price changes that occur continuously or near-continuously in response to changing demand signals, not batch or manual" }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Delivery prices must increase when demand is high relative to a baseline or capacity threshold",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is the core pricing policy. The relationship between demand and price direction is a business strategy decision — a VP could change the formula, thresholds, or sensitivity. It is not a data integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given demand above the defined 'high' threshold, the computed price must be greater than the baseline price"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "Delivery prices must decrease when demand is low relative to a baseline or capacity threshold",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Same as BR-1 — this is a pricing policy direction, not a system integrity constraint. The degree and trigger of decrease are tunable business decisions.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given demand below the defined 'low' threshold, the computed price must be less than the baseline price"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "Price adjustments must occur in real time — the system must react to current demand, not stale or batched data",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "What constitutes 'real time' is a policy/SLA decision (sub-second? sub-minute? per-order?). A business leader could relax this to near-real-time without corrupting state.",
      "testable_assertion": "The time between a demand signal change and the corresponding price update must be within the defined SLA (exact SLA undefined)"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "The pricing system must be fully automated — no manual approval step is required for individual price changes",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Automation level is a business policy. The company could introduce manual overrides or approval gates without corrupting the system.",
      "testable_assertion": "Price adjustments triggered by demand changes must be applied without requiring human intervention or approval"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "Guardrails must constrain prices to prevent them from reaching extreme values — there must be a price ceiling and/or price floor",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Without guardrails, the system could produce prices that are zero, negative, or astronomically high — any of which corrupt the business (giving away service for free or charging impossibly high prices). The existence of bounds is an integrity constraint; the specific bound values are policy.",
      "testable_assertion": "The system must never produce a delivery price outside the configured minimum and maximum bounds"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "A delivery price must always be a positive monetary value greater than zero",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "A zero or negative delivery price is physically and financially nonsensical — it means paying the customer to deliver. This is a fundamental data integrity constraint that no business policy should override.",
      "testable_assertion": "For any demand level and any guardrail configuration, the output price must be > 0"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "The pricing function must be monotonically non-decreasing with respect to demand — higher demand must never produce a lower price than lower demand, all else being equal",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is the implied economic model (price correlates positively with demand). However, a business could introduce non-linear strategies (e.g., loyalty discounts during surge) that break strict monotonicity. It is a strong policy, not a physical constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given two demand levels D1 < D2 with all other inputs equal, price(D2) >= price(D1)"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "The system must balance competitiveness and profitability — prices must not be set so high as to lose all customers, nor so low as to operate at a loss",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a strategic business objective, not a hard system constraint. What constitutes 'competitive' and 'profitable' are subjective, market-dependent judgments.",
      "testable_assertion": "Prices must remain within a configured competitive range and above a configured cost floor (exact values undefined)"
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Demand drops to zero (no orders at all)", "expected_behavior": "Price should hit the configured floor but never reach zero or negative. System should remain operational and ready." },
    { "scenario": "Extreme demand spike (e.g., 100x normal volume in seconds)", "expected_behavior": "Guardrails must cap the price at the ceiling. The system must not overshoot the cap due to latency or race conditions." },
    { "scenario": "Demand oscillates rapidly between high and low", "expected_behavior": "Prices should not 'flicker' causing customer confusion. Some dampening or hysteresis mechanism is likely needed (not specified by CEO)." },
    { "scenario": "System failure or demand signal unavailability", "expected_behavior": "Undefined. Must decide: freeze at last known price? Fall back to baseline? This is a critical gap." },
    { "scenario": "Multiple simultaneous orders at the boundary of a price change", "expected_behavior": "Undefined. Must decide: does the customer see the price at time of quote, or time of confirmation? Price locking semantics are missing." },
    { "scenario": "Guardrail ceiling is set below the cost floor", "expected_behavior": "Configuration error. System must reject or alert on contradictory guardrail settings rather than silently operating at a loss." },
    { "scenario": "Regional or segment-level demand differences", "expected_behavior": "Undefined. Is demand measured globally, per-region, per-service-tier? Granularity of the demand signal is unspecified." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Direct revenue impact — dynamic pricing can significantly increase revenue during peak demand and improve volume during low demand. Miscalibrated guardrails risk either leaving money on the table or driving customers away.",
    "operational": "Requires real-time demand measurement infrastructure, pricing engine with low latency, and monitoring/alerting for anomalies. Adds operational complexity compared to static pricing.",
    "user": "Customers will experience variable pricing, which can erode trust if not communicated transparently. Price volatility may cause confusion, abandoned orders, or perception of unfairness (surge pricing backlash, as seen with ride-sharing).",
    "risk": "Regulatory risk in jurisdictions with price gouging laws (e.g., during natural disasters). Reputational risk from perceived unfairness. Technical risk from automation without human oversight. Financial risk from misconfigured guardrails.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Customers", "impact": "Pay variable prices; may face higher costs during peak times; may benefit from lower prices during off-peak" },
      { "group": "Finance/Revenue team", "impact": "Revenue becomes harder to forecast; need new reporting and margin analysis tooling" },
      { "group": "Operations/Dispatch", "impact": "Pricing signals may influence demand patterns; need to ensure capacity planning aligns with pricing strategy" },
      { "group": "Customer Support", "impact": "Will receive complaints about price variability; need clear policies on refunds/adjustments for price-sensitive orders" },
      { "group": "Legal/Compliance", "impact": "Must review for price gouging regulations, consumer protection laws, and contractual obligations with enterprise clients" },
      { "group": "Couriers/Drivers", "impact": "If courier compensation is tied to delivery price, their earnings become variable; labor relations impact" }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 25,
    "reasoning": "The CEO's description is a high-level vision statement, not a specification. It establishes directional intent (demand-based pricing, automation, guardrails) but leaves nearly every implementable detail undefined: what constitutes 'high' or 'low' demand, the pricing formula, guardrail values, demand signal granularity, failover behavior, price locking semantics, regulatory constraints, and customer communication strategy. The word 'probably' before guardrails further undermines confidence. This cannot be implemented without substantial follow-up specification.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Price goes up when demand is high (directionally clear)",
      "Price goes down when demand is low (directionally clear)",
      "System is automated (explicitly stated)",
      "Prices must be positive (implied by physical reality of delivery services)"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Guardrail specifics — CEO said 'probably', not 'definitely'",
      "Definition of 'high' and 'low' demand — no thresholds given",
      "Real-time definition — latency SLA completely undefined",
      "Pricing formula — linear? exponential? tiered? unknown",
      "Demand signal granularity — global, regional, per-zone, per-service?",
      "Failure mode behavior — no mention of fallback strategy",
      "Price locking and quote validity — no mention",
      "Competitive and profitability thresholds — subjective terms with no quantification",
      "Regulatory compliance — not mentioned at all",
      "Customer communication of dynamic prices — not addressed"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "Guardrails will exist despite the CEO's 'probably' qualifier — a system without them is operationally dangerous",
      "Delivery price must always be positive — inferred from the nature of delivery services, not stated",
      "The pricing relationship to demand is monotonically non-decreasing — inferred from 'high demand = high price' but not explicitly guaranteed",
      "The system applies to customer-facing delivery prices, not internal cost allocation",
      "There is a baseline or reference price that adjustments are relative to"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The CEO's statement 'We'll probably have some guardrails' is non-committal. Are guardrails a hard requirement or optional? Without clarity, we cannot determine whether BR-5 is in scope.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Engineering", "Product", "Legal/Compliance", "Customers"],
      "options": ["Treat guardrails as mandatory (safer default)", "Treat guardrails as optional and build without them initially", "Require explicit confirmation from CEO/Product"],
      "recommendation": "Treat guardrails as mandatory — the risk of unbounded automated pricing is too high. But get explicit confirmation."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No pricing formula, demand thresholds, guardrail values, demand signal definition, or failure mode behavior are specified. This vision statement cannot be implemented without a detailed product specification.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Engineering", "Product", "Finance", "Operations"],
      "options": ["Request a full product specification from Product team", "Run a product discovery workshop to define the missing parameters", "Build a prototype with placeholder values and iterate"],
      "recommendation": "Request a full product specification that defines: demand measurement methodology, pricing formula, guardrail bounds, update frequency SLA, failure mode behavior, price locking semantics, and regional granularity."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No mention of regulatory or legal considerations. Dynamic pricing in delivery/logistics may be subject to price gouging laws, consumer protection regulations, or contractual constraints with enterprise clients.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Legal/Compliance", "Enterprise Sales", "Customers"],
      "options": ["Proceed without legal review and add compliance later", "Block implementation until legal review is complete"],
      "recommendation": "Legal review must happen before launch. Identify jurisdictions of operation and applicable regulations. This could fundamentally constrain guardrail values and pricing behavior."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "What does 'competitive' mean? Against whom? Based on what data? Is competitor pricing an input signal alongside demand, or is this purely a strategic aspiration?",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Product", "Strategy", "Finance"],
      "options": ["Ignore competitiveness as a system input and treat it as a guardrail calibration exercise", "Build competitor price monitoring into the system as an input signal"],
      "recommendation": "Clarify with Product whether 'competitive' is a design aspiration (addressed through guardrail tuning) or an active system input (requiring competitor price data feeds)."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
